text: The patient underwent cerebral angiography which confirmed the findings.
Revascularization of the left vertebral artery was attempted, but the lesion was unable to be crossed.
Transcranial Doppler monitoring was performed throughout the procedure to monitor cerebral flow as well as identify any cerebral embolisms during surgical maneuvers.
A midline hemi sternotomy was performed with planned extension of the incision anterior to the right sternocleidomastoid muscle.
The sternotomy was teed off into the third rib inner space to allow for exposure of the innominate artery.
A robust thymus was encountered and mobilized.
Following this, the left brachiocephalic vein was dissected free and controlled with an umbilical tape and retracted caudally.
We continued to extend our incision anterior to the sternocleidomastoid muscle.
All veins and lymphatics were ligated with silk ties.
Dissection was continued between the strap muscles and the sternal head of the sternocleidomastoid muscle.
The right common carotid artery was identified and skeletonized with care to avoid injury to the vagus nerve.
We continued our dissection cephalad and identified the facial vein which was ligated with silk ties as well.
The ancestor of the callus nerve was identified and protected.
We turn our attention to exposing the innominate artery.
The inferior belly of the omohyoid was ligated.
We identified the densely calcified distal innominate artery and carefully skeletonized the innominate artery with care to avoid injury to the recurrent laryngeal nerve.
The innominate artery was controlled with an umbilical tape.
A clamp was brought to the field to test our clamping location.
Following this, we simultaneously exposed the right carotid artery bifurcation and the right axillary artery through an infraclavicular incision.
In the infraclavicular incision, dissection was carried through the deltopectoral groove.
The pectoralis minor muscle was divided with electrocautery.
During the carotid bulb manipulation, we did not identify any embolic hits on transcranial doppler monitoring.
A tunnel was created from the axillary incision to the neck.
The patient was systemically heparinized at this point.
Next, the innominate artery was clamped.
The carotid artery was clamped and there was mild drop in the MCA flow following this maneuver, which was fully compensated by raising the systolic blood pressure.
The innominate artery was transected and the distal stump was endarterectomized in order to suture ligate the stump.
Following this, the proximal stump was endarterectomized to prepare for the proximal anastomosis.
A 14x7 bifurcated graft was brought to the field and the proximal anastomosis was performed with a running 4-row proline suture in a parachute fashion.
The graph was checked for any leaks using saline.
The clamp was released and satisfactory hemostasis was achieved.
The right limb of the graft was tunneled to the axillary incision.
The right common carotid artery was transected.
The proximal stump was endarterectomized and again suture ligated.
The graft was cut to length and an end to end anastomosis was performed with a running pulling stitch in a similar fashion.
Meanwhile, an end to side anastomosis was performed between the right limb of the graft and the right axillary artery.
The left limb of the graft was vented by releasing the common carotid artery clamp and back bleeding towards the axillary anastomosis.
The axillary limb was reclamped and the carotid clamp was replaced onto the left limb of the graft.
Following this, a standard carotid endarterectomy was performed with patch angioplasty.
After completion, the carotid clamp was released while clamping the internal carotid artery with forceps to allow debris to flow into the external carotid artery.
The internal carotid artery was released and there was a marked improvement in the right MCA flow with a sharp upstroke.
Hemostasis was achieved.
There was noted to be a good lie of the graph without any kinking or twisting.
A 24 French Blake train was brought in from the lateral neck and tunneled along the graft.
The sternum was closed with wires with care to prevent any compression of the newly constructed graft.
Following this, the fascia was closed with a running PDS.
and the skin was closed with a subcuticular monocural stitch.
